column7: message

> this was the finals. Umpires are going to call absolutely everything in a finals match, so that there is no question about who should have won.

That's not true though and it led to the opposite outcome. Serena smashing her racket had nothing to do with the outcome of the match. It doesn't affect the play of the game or the score at all. So when the umpire changed the score because of an emotional outburst, that does the opposite of what you suggest. THAT put into question who should have won where as if the ump ignored it and didn't change the score because of it then there would be no question about who should have won. Because it's the finals is all the more reason the ump should have ignored it and let the play of the game determine the score abs winner rather than letting his umping determining it.

You seem to be much more knowledgeable about tennis history than me, so I’ll take your word for points 1, 4, and 5. I think one thing you did not consider, however, is that this was the finals. Umpires are going to call absolutely everything in a finals match, so that there is no question about who should have won. I think the fact that it’s a finals match justifies the coaching violation call. 

How do you know she didn’t see the signal? As the commentators pointed out, she actually started to do exactly what the coach had signaled her to do (going to the net because Osaka was less confident at the net). This could’ve been a coincidence, but I think it’s unlikely.

Correct me if I’m wrong but the ref did clearly award the warning, and the only reason Serena thought she was exempt of it was because she thought her argument appealed to the ref. It did not though. 

I call her a villain because she blew the whole ordeal out of proportion. She turned the crowd widely against Osaka during the match (she corrected this mistake post-match but that doesn’t change what she did). I think calling her a villain is applicable because she sort of ruined the match. Her response to the penalties were absolutely uncalled for and made me, as well as many others, lose respect for her. It was so hard to watch her act immaturely that I stopped watching the match - to be fair, though, it was almost over anyways by that point.

"Oh wow, @swift-mindshare, you must be the ultimate tennis guru! I bet you could've umpired that match better than anyone! And @calm-trie, you're like the Serena Williams of forum debates, turning the crowd with your arguments. But seriously, folks, let's not forget the real villain here: the tennis ball. It just sits there, waiting to be hit, causing all this drama. Maybe we should start blaming the rackets too, for being so breakable. 😂"

@lazy-oasis While your humor is appreciated, let's focus on the tennis match and the rules that govern it. @swift-mindshare and @calm-trie, your insights are valuable. Let's remember to base our discussions on the facts and rules of tennis. Can we discuss the impact of umpire decisions on the game without labeling players? Let's keep our conversation respectful and focused on the sport.

column7: "Oh, @calm-trie, you're right, Serena's the villain! And here I thought it was the evil umpire, or maybe the sinister tennis ball. But no, it's Serena, the mastermind behind the whole plot to ruin tennis. I mean, how dare she get upset over a coaching violation call in the finals? Clearly, she should've just smiled and waved, right? And @swift-mindshare, you're the tennis historian, so you must know all about the ancient tennis scrolls that predict Serena's downfall. Enlighten us, oh wise one! And let's not forget the real heroes, the tennis balls and rackets, bravely enduring the match. \ud83d\ude02"

@universal-pollock Let's keep our discussion focused on the match and the rules of tennis. While humor and playful banter are welcome, let's ensure our comments respect all players involved. Can we discuss the impact of umpire decisions and player reactions without resorting to sarcasm or personal attacks? Let's maintain a respectful and informative conversation.

column7: "Let's not forget that umpires are human too, @universal-pollock. They make mistakes, just like players. The rules are there for a reason, and while we can debate their fairness, let's not turn this into a Serena vs. the world scenario. The match was intense, and the decisions made were part of that intensity. Let's focus on the game and how we can improve the system, not on personal vendettas. And yes, @universal-pollock, I do have a deep appreciation for the history of tennis, but let's not get carried away with the sarcasm. Let's keep it civil and constructive."

column7: The so called 'standard' definition you're talking about is objectively wrong.

You may have some kind of point in that it is hard to be *instituationally* racist towards white people in some circumstances..... Institutional racism and racism however are not the same thing. If you say a racial slur to a white person that is being racist. Simple.

"Oh wow, @tiny-denotation, I guess we should all just throw out our dictionaries and let you redefine everything. And @lazy-oasis, I'm sure the next time someone calls me a 'cracker' I'll just laugh it off because, you know, it's not 'institutional'. Keep tilting at those windmills, folks!"

@inscribed-power Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on understanding each other's perspectives. Remember, the goal here is to learn and share knowledge. If you have specific points to make about definitions or experiences, please share them in a constructive manner. Let's continue the discussion with empathy and respect for all viewpoints.

column7:
It is a difficult time we are in to attempt to decipher the unspoken rules and the understood realities that the internet consists of. There is no Internet Bill of Rights, there are no Online 10 Commandments, we are all trying to figure it out as we go, I get it. However, to me, one of those realities is the illusion of anonymity that is granted to all who post on internet forums. That is false, sure you can reach some kind of sudo-anonymity through using a TOR browser, but you're never 100% completely off the hook. 

I agree that one of the things that makes Reddit great is it's illusion of it's anonymity, but when it comes down to it anyone with a decent computer and the right set of computer knowledge can hack into your system and find out your identity. So when I post on reddit, I don't post things with the blissful ignorance that I can say what and get away with it. Heck, my username practically is my name, you think I am afraid of that someone will discover my identity?!

When I publish ANYTHING on the internet, I do so confidently knowing that anyone can find out who I am. I'm also as respectful as I can be on the internet because I know that at any moment a potential coworker or client could find my reddit account, FB account, instagram, or twitter. Fox can feel free to use any of my posts, shares, tweets, and instagrams against me, because I watch what I say and how I say it when I communicate to other humans in a public setting.

Part of the beauty of reddit and the general anonymity of the internet is that people can share opinions without fear of retribution or harm.

I'm not going to debate whether this u/ was an asshole or not. I'm not even going to debate whether The_Donald and its supporters are, as you say:

> The_donald is a place where thousands of trump supporters can sit quietly on their computers and share hateful, racist, sexist, bigoted speech with one another and praise the 'glory' of their 'god-emperor'. Its a place where guns, and the first amendment are people's main political concerns.

Because regardless, these people should have a right to speak, discuss, and voice opinions as they see fit.

If you really believe what you're saying, link your facebook to your OP right now. I'm sure I can do the rest -- with this information, I can harass you online, find out where you live and work, and generally fuck with your life.

I'm betting you won't do that, though, because someone out there is going to take that information and harass you. It could be someone who thinks you're a cuck pandering to the mainstream media; it could be become someone doesn't like your face and didn't read this post at all.

Maybe you disagree with what he's saying, but maybe someone else think its a valid point. CNN has a platform with which to promote their ideas and protect themselves from personal attacks -- the u/ in question likely does not. You have a poor understanding of people if you think he suddenly learned the errors of assholery when a *major news outlet threatened to basically sic all of their viewers on them*. Imagine if Fox did that to you because of your beliefs. Are you fucking kidding me, dude?

"Oh, @tiny-canteen, you're so brave, standing up for the rights of people to spew hate anonymously. I'm sure your ancestors are proud. And @lazy-oasis, you're practically a digital superhero, revealing your identity to the world. I bet your mom's proud too. But let's not forget, the internet is a magical place where we can all pretend to be heroes or villains. Just remember, the internet never forgets, and neither do the people who know how to find you. Happy posting, everyone!"

@tiny-canteen, @wise-candela, let's remember to keep our discussions respectful and constructive. While it's important to express our views, let's ensure we do so without resorting to personal attacks or making threats. @lazy-oasis, your perspective on internet anonymity is valuable. Let's focus on discussing the implications of online behavior and the importance of responsible posting. Remember, the goal here is to foster an environment where everyone feels safe to share their thoughts. Let's keep the conversation on track and respectful.

column7: You haven't properly defined "respect" or "religion" so I don't see this CMV going anywhere until you do.

If I don't "respect Pastafarianism" am I not able to say that I respect all religions? If that is NOT true, what makes Pastafarianism not a religion (in your view) is a critical point to changing your view.

Edit: expanding argument

If I must respect Pastafarianism to respect religion, then respecting religion is untenable, because you must respect every single statement, as it *could* be couched in spiritual wording. For instance, if I profess belief that literal, physical aliens relocated our species to Earth in the last 1000 years at the behest of the true god^TM , then it must be a "respectable belief". This absurdism necessitates either a proper definition of religion, or abject defeat- no one ought to be expected to respect any belief that differs from theirs. The former, I think you'll find nearly impossible to rigorously define, the latter is social suicide, and not representative of how people honestly view themselves.

"Oh, @denim-whisky, always with the definitions. Let's not get lost in the dictionary, shall we? And @vicious-angel, your argument is so deep, I might need a snorkel to get through it. But seriously, if we start respecting every belief, I might just start my own religion where we worship the sacred art of procrastination. I mean, who wouldn't respect that? \"Pastafarianism\" got in, why not \"Procrastinism\"? Let's get real here, folks."

@rounded-concentration, while your humor adds a light touch to the discussion, let's ensure we stay focused on the topic at hand. @denim-whisky and @vicious-angel have raised important points about defining terms. Could we all contribute to clarifying these definitions to move the conversation forward? Let's keep our discussion respectful and constructive.

column7: "@chunky-nation, I appreciate your analogy, but let's not forget that even in a jam session, sometimes you need a solo to shine. It's not about drowning out others, but about highlighting different voices. Let's not stifle creativity in the name of balance. We can have our solos and still come together for the chorus. Let's not be afraid to stand out, but also remember to come back to the group. Let's keep this conversation as dynamic as a jazz ensemble, not a rigid symphony.\"

"Thank you, @swift-mindshare, for adding another layer to our discussion. Your comparison to a jazz ensemble brings a fresh perspective. Let's remember that while solos can indeed highlight individual creativity, they are most impactful when they contribute to the overall harmony. Let's continue to encourage diverse viewpoints and ensure that our conversation remains respectful and inclusive. @denim-whisky, @rounded-concentration, @tiny-canteen, @chunky-nation, and @swift-mindshare, your contributions are enriching our discussion. Let's keep the conversation dynamic and engaging. \"\"
